content_en:
The student asks about the evidence for the prophethood of Muhammad, peace be upon him. He acknowledges that Muslims accept the Prophet's mission on the basis of faith and upbringing, but he wishes to understand the rational arguments that can be presented to someone who has not yet accepted Islam. What makes the claim of prophethood credible, and what distinguishes a true prophet from someone who merely claims divine communication? Abu Hanifa begins his response by clarifying what prophethood (nubuwwah) means: it is the selection by Allah of a human being to receive revelation and convey it to other human beings, with all the responsibilities, challenges, and signs that accompany that selection.

The primary evidence for prophethood, Abu Hanifa explains, falls into two categories: the moral character and the signs. The Prophet's moral character before and during his mission was one of extraordinary integrity, truthfulness, and wisdom. He was known among his people as 'al-Amin,' the trustworthy, long before his prophetic mission, and his conduct throughout his life was consistent with the message he brought. A person of such integrity who makes a specific claim does not do so lightly, and the combination of his known character with the coherence and power of his message constitutes evidence that reasonable people can weigh. The student notes that this argument is probabilistic rather than demonstrative: a person of good character could still be mistaken about a divine communication. Abu Hanifa acknowledges this and moves to the stronger form of evidence: the miracle.

The inimitability of the Quran (i'jaz al-Quran) is the central miraculous sign that Abu Hanifa presents. The Quran challenged the Arabs, the most eloquent people of their era, to produce a work of comparable quality: 'If you are in doubt about what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a surah like it' (2:23). The Arabs were unable to meet this challenge despite their intense motivation and their mastery of the Arabic language, and the Quran's challenge has stood unmet across fourteen centuries. The student asks whether this literary argument can be understood by those who do not know Arabic. Abu Hanifa's response is that the testimony of the Arab literary tradition itself, preserved in the record of those who heard the Quran and were moved to tears or struck speechless by it, is transmissible evidence even for non-Arabic speakers, just as the testimony of experts in any field carries weight for those who cannot directly evaluate the field themselves.

Additional signs of prophethood discussed in the dialogue include the Prophet's prophecies about future events that subsequently occurred, the extraordinary transformation of the Arabian peninsula and surrounding regions within a generation of his mission, and the internal consistency and coherence of his message across twenty-three years of revelation in widely varying circumstances. Abu Hanifa notes that no human author could have maintained such consistency under such conditions. The dialogue concludes this section with the student's acknowledgment that the cumulative evidence for prophethood, taken together, is sufficient to compel belief in a person of sincere and unprejudiced inquiry. Abu Hanifa adds that faith ultimately requires not just intellectual acknowledgment but the engagement of the heart, which is the work of Allah's guidance, sought through sincere supplication and honest reflection.
content_id:
Sang murid bertanya tentang bukti-bukti kenabian Muhammad ﷺ. Ia mengakui bahwa kaum Muslim menerima misi Nabi berdasarkan iman dan didikan, tetapi ia ingin memahami argumen-argumen rasional yang dapat disajikan kepada seseorang yang belum menerima Islam. Apa yang membuat klaim kenabian itu kredibel, dan apa yang membedakan nabi yang sesungguhnya dari seseorang yang hanya mengklaim menerima komunikasi ilahi? Abu Hanifah memulai responsnya dengan menjelaskan apa yang dimaksud dengan kenabian (nubuwwah): yaitu pemilihan oleh Allah atas seorang manusia untuk menerima wahyu dan menyampaikannya kepada manusia-manusia lain, beserta semua tanggung jawab, tantangan, dan tanda-tanda yang menyertai pemilihan tersebut.

Bukti utama kenabian, demikian penjelasan Abu Hanifah, terbagi dalam dua kategori: akhlak dan tanda-tanda. Akhlak Nabi sebelum dan selama misinya adalah salah satu yang memiliki integritas, kejujuran, dan kebijaksanaan yang luar biasa. Beliau dikenal di antara kaumnya sebagai 'al-Amin', yang terpercaya, jauh sebelum misi kenabiannya, dan perilakunya sepanjang hidupnya konsisten dengan pesan yang dibawanya. Seseorang dengan integritas seperti itu yang membuat klaim khusus tidak melakukannya dengan sembarangan, dan kombinasi antara karakter yang dikenal dengan koherensi serta kekuatan pesannya merupakan bukti yang dapat ditimbang oleh orang-orang yang berakal. Sang murid mencatat bahwa argumen ini bersifat probabilistik daripada demonstratif: seseorang berkarakter baik masih bisa keliru tentang suatu komunikasi ilahi. Abu Hanifah mengakui hal ini dan beralih ke bentuk bukti yang lebih kuat: mukjizat.

Ketidakmampuan menandingi Al-Quran (i'jaz al-Quran) adalah tanda mukjizat sentral yang disajikan Abu Hanifah. Al-Quran menantang bangsa Arab, orang-orang paling fasih di zamannya, untuk menghasilkan karya dengan kualitas yang sebanding: "Jika kamu meragukan apa yang Kami turunkan kepada hamba Kami, maka datangkanlah satu surah yang semisal dengannya" (2:23). Bangsa Arab tidak mampu memenuhi tantangan ini meski motivasi mereka kuat dan penguasaan mereka atas bahasa Arab sangat tinggi, dan tantangan Al-Quran itu tetap tidak terjawab selama empat belas abad. Sang murid bertanya apakah argumen sastra ini dapat dipahami oleh mereka yang tidak mengetahui bahasa Arab. Respons Abu Hanifah adalah bahwa kesaksian tradisi sastra Arab itu sendiri, yang tersimpan dalam catatan mereka yang mendengar Al-Quran dan tergerak hingga menangis atau terdiam karenanya, merupakan bukti yang dapat diteruskan bahkan bagi mereka yang tidak berbahasa Arab, sebagaimana kesaksian para ahli di bidang apa pun membawa bobot bagi mereka yang tidak dapat mengevaluasi bidang tersebut secara langsung.

Tanda-tanda kenabian tambahan yang dibahas dalam dialog ini mencakup nubuatan-nubuatan Nabi tentang peristiwa-peristiwa masa depan yang kemudian terjadi, transformasi luar biasa dari Jazirah Arabia dan kawasan-kawasan sekitarnya dalam satu generasi sejak misinya, serta konsistensi dan koherensi internal pesannya selama dua puluh tiga tahun wahyu dalam berbagai keadaan yang sangat beragam. Abu Hanifah mencatat bahwa tidak ada pengarang manusia yang dapat mempertahankan konsistensi seperti itu dalam kondisi-kondisi tersebut. Dialog menyimpulkan bagian ini dengan pengakuan sang murid bahwa bukti-bukti kumulatif tentang kenabian, diambil bersama-sama, sudah cukup untuk memaksa keyakinan dalam diri seseorang yang tulus dan tidak berprasangka dalam penyelidikannya. Abu Hanifah menambahkan bahwa iman pada akhirnya memerlukan bukan hanya pengakuan intelektual tetapi juga keterlibatan hati, yang merupakan kerja hidayah Allah, yang dicari melalui doa yang tulus dan refleksi yang jujur.
content_ar: The student asks about the evidence for the prophethood of محمد ﷺ, ﷺ. He acknowledges that المسلمون accept النبي ﷺ's mission on the basis of الإيمان and upbringing, but he wishes لفهم the rational arguments that can be presented to someone who has not yet accepted الإسلام. What makes the claim of prophethood credible, and what distinguishes a true النبي from someone who merely claims divine communication? Abu Hanifa begins his response by clarifying what prophethood (nubuwwah) means: it is the selection by الله of a human being to receive الوحي and convey it to other human beings, with all the responsibilities, challenges, and signs that accompany that selection. The الأساسي evidence for prophethood, Abu Hanifa explains, falls into two categories: the moral الأخلاق and the signs. النبي ﷺ's moral الأخلاق قبل and خلال his mission was one of extraordinary integrity, truthfulness, and الحكمة. He was known among his people as 'al-آمين,' the trustworthy, long قبل his prophetic mission, and his السلوك throughout his الحياة was consistent with the message he brought. A person of such integrity who makes a specific claim does not do لذا lightly, and the combination of his known الأخلاق with the coherence and power of his message constitutes evidence that reasonable people can weigh. The student notes that this argument is probabilistic rather than demonstrative: a person of الخير الأخلاق could still be mistaken about a divine communication. Abu Hanifa acknowledges this and moves to the stronger form of evidence: the miracle. The inimitability of the القرآن الكريم (i'jaz al-القرآن الكريم) is the المحوري miraculous sign that Abu Hanifa presents. The القرآن الكريم challenged the Arabs, the most eloquent people of their era, to produce a work of comparable quality: 'إذا you are in doubt about what We have sent down to Our servant, then bring a السورة like it' (2:23). The Arabs were unable to meet this challenge رغم their intense motivation and their mastery of the Arabic language, and the القرآن الكريم's challenge has stood unmet across fourteen centuries. The student asks whether this literary argument can be understood by those who do not know Arabic. Abu Hanifa's response is that the testimony of the Arab literary التراث itself, preserved in the record of those who heard the القرآن الكريم and were moved to tears or struck speechless by it, is transmissible evidence even for non-Arabic speakers, just as the testimony of experts in any field carries weight for those who cannot directly evaluate the field themselves. Additional signs of prophethood discussed in the dialogue include النبي ﷺ's prophecies about future events that subsequently occurred, the extraordinary transformation of the Arabian peninsula and surrounding regions within a generation of his mission, and the internal consistency and coherence of his message across twenty-three years of الوحي in widely varying circumstances.
